+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 83.2
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
It is a generally accepted fact among those who survived the End of the World that the apocalypse was, above all else, incredibly inconvenient. The oceans boiling and the grid collapsing were certainly bad, but the real tragedy was the sudden, permanent cessation of indoor plumbing. Midas sat in the rusted-out shell of a 2006 Ford Aerostar, which he affectionately called ‘Home Base,’ and stared at a map drawn in charcoal on the back of a radiation-warning poster. "The way I see it," Midas said, rubbing a smudge of soot from his nose, "if we skirt the Crater of Unfortunate Smells and take the old highway overpass—the bit that isn't currently on fire—we can reach the fabled Super-Mart in under two hours." Next to him sat Chewy. Chewy was, technically speaking, an animatronic stuffed koala from the Before Time. Time, weather, and a brief altercation with a feral badger had left Chewy looking less like a cuddly marsupial and more like a bundle of synthetic fur that had been dragged through a hedge backwards, set on fire, and lovingly extinguished with a brick. One of his plastic eyes was missing; the other burned with a dull, red, demonic LED glow. "We are going for peaches," Midas told the bear, tapping the map. "Canned peaches, Chewy. Sweet, syrupy, non-radioactive peaches. I am tired of eating lichen. Lichen tastes like despair." Chewy’s internal gears ground together with the sound of a coffee grinder digesting a handful of gravel. His jaw snapped open. *“Ask your doctor if Zoloft is right for you!”* Chewy chirped in a tinny, hyper-cheerful voice. "I would," Midas sighed, "but Dr. Henderson was eaten by a mutant pelican last Tuesday. Let’s move." Midas was a man who possessed a supernatural affinity for disaster. If it was raining, the only cloud in the sky would center itself firmly over Midas’s head. If there was a single landmine left in a fifty-mile radius, Midas could be reliably counted upon to find it with his toe. He was, therefore, uniquely unsuited for the post-apocalypse, and yet, here he was, entirely due to the fact that Death probably found him too exhausting to deal with. The trek to the Super-Mart began with a cautious optimism that was immediately dashed when Midas stepped into a puddle that defied all known laws of geometry by being three feet deeper than it appeared. Soaking wet up to his thigh, he limped onward, Chewy strapped securely to his back in a makeshift papoose. The landscape was a rusted graveyard of the Before. Skeletal remains of shopping carts roamed the plains, driven by the wind. The sky was the exact color of a television tuned to a dead channel, assuming anyone still alive remembered what a television was. Presently, they arrived at the Super-Mart. Half of the roof had caved in, and the sliding glass doors were permanently wedged open, framing a dark, gaping maw of retail opportunity. "Alright," Midas whispered, crouching behind a desiccated Honda Civic. "We go in, we find Aisle 4—Canned Goods and Preserves—we take the peaches, and we leave. Quietly. No sudden movements." *“Buy one, get one FREE! While supplies last!”* Chewy announced to the devastated parking lot. "Shh!" Midas hissed, swatting blindly over his shoulder. Midas crept inside. The gloom was heavy, smelling of dust and expired floor wax. He moved with the exaggerated stealth of a man who knows the universe is actively watching him, waiting for a punchline. He navigated past overturned displays of sunglasses and a skeletal cashier still wearing a blue vest. And then, he saw it. At the end of Aisle 4, bathed in a solitary shaft of sunlight piercing through the ruined roof, sat a pristine, dent-free can. The label was faded, but the image of the plump, orange fruit was unmistakable. Peaches. Midas’s heart soared. He took a step forward. *CRUNCH.* Midas looked down. He had stepped on a dried twig. It was a curious thing to find in Aisle 4, but Midas didn't have time to ponder its origins, because the shadows at the end of the aisle began to move. It wasn't a mutant badger. It wasn't a band of cannibalistic accountants. It was worse. It was the Geese. Before the Collapse, geese were merely ill-tempered water-fowl. After the Collapse, bathed in a cocktail of strange chemicals and unfiltered cosmic rays, they had grown to the size of large dogs, retaining all of their original malice but now equipped with serrated beaks and a deep, abiding hatred for humanity. Three of them stepped into the light. They stared at Midas with eyes like black beads of pure hatred. "Nice birdies," Midas squeaked, slowly reaching his hand out toward the canned peaches. The lead goose opened its beak and hissed—a sound like a tire violently deflating. *“Nothing outruns a V8 engine!”* Chewy suddenly bellowed from Midas’s back, at maximum volume. The geese charged. "Oh, for the love of—!" Midas snatched the can from the shelf, shoved it into his satchel, and turned on his heel. What followed was less a tactical retreat and more a masterclass in chaotic physics. Midas sprinted down Aisle 3, pursued by a chorus of demonic honking. He threw a display of breath mints behind him. The geese vaulted it effortlessly. He scrambled up a leaning tower of toilet paper, which immediately collapsed, sending him surfing on a wave of two-ply toward the pharmacy section. *“Apply directly to the forehead! Apply directly to the forehead!”* Chewy chanted helpfully as Midas took a glancing blow to the skull from a falling shelf. Midas scrambled to his feet, burst through the emergency exit, and sprinted into the wasteland. The geese pursued him for exactly half a mile before deciding he wasn't worth the calories, but Midas didn't stop running until he tripped over a rusted fire hydrant, tumbled down a steep embankment of scree, and landed face-first in a patch of stinging nettles. Hours later, the sun dipped below the horizon, painting the smoggy sky in vibrant shades of toxic orange. Midas crawled back into the Ford Aerostar. He was missing his left shoe. His jacket was torn in three places, his face was swollen from the nettles, and he was bleeding sluggishly from a goose-peck on his shoulder. He collapsed onto his sleeping bag, groaning as his bruised ribs protested. He unstrapped Chewy and set the battered koala on the dashboard. "We made it," Midas wheezed, tasting copper and dirt. "We actually made it. It went entirely sideways, I think I fractured my shin, and I may have contracted avian rabies, but we got the peaches." With trembling, bloodied hands, Midas unclasped his satchel. He reached inside and pulled out the prize. He held it up to the fading light, anticipating the sweet, sugary reward of his perilous labor. Midas stopped. He blinked his swollen eyes. He wiped the dust from the faded label. It was not a can of peaches. In the dim light of the Super-Mart, he had been deceived by the color. The label depicted a cheerful, orange bowl of something entirely different. *Chef Boyardee’s Beef Ravioli in Tomato Sauce.* Midas stared at the can for a long, profound minute. The silence in the Aerostar was absolute, save for the distant howling of the wind and the gentle dripping of Midas's dignity. Chewy’s remaining red eye flickered. Gears whirred in the silence. *“I’m lovin’ it!”* the koala declared. Midas slowly lowered his head onto his knees and began to weep.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive style, successfully capturing his dry wit, observational humor, and philosophical asides about human nature. The opening line about the apocalypse being "incredibly inconvenient" immediately establishes the Pratchettesque tone, and the author maintains this voice consistently throughout. The story structure adheres well to the prompt requirements: it begins with deliberation over the mission, details how everything goes wrong, and returns the protagonists home "worse for wear." The characterization is effective - Midas as a disaster magnet feels authentic and his relationship with the malfunctioning animatronic koala creates genuine humor through their contrasting natures. The world-building is economical but vivid, painting a post-apocalyptic landscape with memorable details like "shopping carts roaming the plains" and the "Crater of Unfortunate Smells." The author shows restraint in not over-explaining the collapse, letting details emerge naturally. The action sequence with the mutant geese is well-paced and genuinely funny, escalating absurdly while maintaining internal logic. The dialogue, particularly Chewy's inappropriate advertising slogans, provides consistent comic relief without becoming repetitive. However, the piece has some minor weaknesses. Certain descriptions occasionally veer toward the overwrought ("eyes like black beads of pure hatred"), and some metaphors feel slightly forced. The ending, while appropriately deflating, could have been more emotionally complex - the simple disappointment feels somewhat one-note. The prose flows naturally and maintains reader engagement throughout. The author successfully balances humor with genuine pathos, particularly in Midas's final moment of defeat. The piece feels complete and satisfying within its word count constraints. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 13 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 15.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 13.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5.0, Amateurish: 4.0, Purple Prose: 6.0, Overwrought: 7.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 1.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 82.0
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The brass bell above the door of *Llyfrau Arian* jingled with violent urgency. The heavy oak door slammed shut, cutting off the rhythmic popping of camera flashes and the relentless drumming of the Welsh rain. Julian Vance pressed his back against the frosted glass, chest heaving, water dripping from the ends of his famously tousled hair. He waited for the inevitable gasp. The dropped book. The trembling request for an autograph. Instead, a dry voice floated from behind a stack of antiquarian encyclopedias. "The sign on the door says 'Ar Gau'. That means closed." Julian blinked, wiping rain from his eyes. He stepped around the precarious towers of books to find a woman sitting behind a mahogany counter. She wore a thick, oversized cardigan and a pair of tortoiseshell glasses pushed up into her dark hair. She didn't look up. She was entirely focused on a small, disassembled metal tube under a bright desk lamp. He flashed his trademark, million-dollar smile—the one that had recently secured him the title of *People’s* Sexiest Man Alive. "Sorry about that, darling. It’s an absolute zoo out there. I just need a minute to let the hounds lose my scent." "The hounds," she repeated, finally glancing up. Her eyes were a striking, clear hazel, but they held zero recognition. Only mild annoyance. "The paparazzi," Julian clarified, stepping closer, leaning an elbow on the counter. The smile widened, crinkling the corners of his eyes just the way his director had told him was 'devastating.' "Julian. Julian Vance." "Congratulations, Julian Vance. You're trespassing." Julian’s smile faltered. He let out a breathy, charming laugh, assuming she was playing a game. "Right. Good one. Seriously, though, they’ve been chasing me since Cardiff. You wouldn't throw me back out to the wolves, would you?" "I might. The wolves seem quite keen, and you're dripping all over my first-edition Dylan Thomas." Julian quickly stepped back from the display table, genuinely flustered. He ran a hand through his damp hair, a gesture that usually made his co-stars swoon. "Look, I'm sorry. I just—do you really not know who I am? Or are you just doing that thing where you pretend not to care to keep me grounded?" Carys kept her face perfectly blank, though beneath the heavy wool of her cardigan, her heart was hammering a frantic, entirely un-Welsh rhythm against her ribs. Of course she knew who he was. He was Julian Vance. He was currently starring in the blockbuster spy thriller playing at the cinema down the street, and he was somehow twice as breathtaking in person, smelling of expensive cologne and petrichor. But she would rather swallow a live toad than let him see the effect he was having on her. "I can assure you," Carys said, her voice a cool, lilting purr, "I have no interest in your grounding. I'm merely trying to fix this." Julian’s ego, a delicate and highly demanding creature, flared. He wasn't used to indifference. He *needed* to be liked. It was a pathological flaw that made him an excellent actor and a terrible recluse. He leaned back in, dropping the suave leading-man routine for something more earnest and slightly desperate. "Okay, come on. Is it my hair? I know it's a mess. Or did you see *The Corsair's Gambit*? Because I swear, the script was butchered in editing. I’m actually a very respectable actor." Carys felt a treacherous flutter in her stomach at his sudden vulnerability. He looked like a scolded golden retriever. She forced her attention back to her hands, picking up a pair of fine tweezers. "I’m sure you’re very respectable, Mr. Vance. But unless you're here to buy a book on medieval Welsh agriculture, I'm afraid you're interrupting my work." Julian stared at her. His charm offensive had hit a brick wall, and paradoxically, it was the most intoxicating thing he’d experienced in months. Everyone always agreed with him. Everyone always laughed at his jokes. This woman in a dusty bookshop in the middle of nowhere was completely immune to him. He leaned closer, squinting at the desk lamp. "What are you doing, anyway? It looks like you're performing surgery on a torpedo." Carys sighed, her tweezers hovering. "It's a fountain pen. A 1920s Waterman safety pen. The cork seal has rotted, and I am trying to replace it without cracking the hard rubber." Julian froze. The desperate need for approval vanished from his face, replaced by genuine, sharp interest. "A Waterman 42? Red ripple ebonite?" Carys’s hand jerked, and she dropped the tweezers. She stared at him, her carefully constructed mask of indifference slipping. "How do you know that?" Julian barked a real, unpolished laugh, his eyes lighting up. "Are you kidding? I collect them. Well, I restore them. It’s... a thing I do. Keeps my hands busy on set when I’m stuck in a trailer for twelve hours." He gestured toward the desk. "You’re using cork? Have you tried using rubber O-rings instead? They last longer and you don't have to boil them in paraffin." "O-rings aren't historically accurate," Carys said, her voice breathless, completely thrown off balance. "No, but they won't leak India ink all over your shirt pocket during a premiere, either," Julian countered, sliding onto the stool opposite her. He pointed at the tiny mechanism. "You're struggling with the spiral groove, aren't you? The pin is catching." "Yes," Carys admitted, her cheeks suddenly warm. She looked at his hands—long-fingered, surprisingly calloused. "It’s been driving me mad for two hours." "May I?" Julian asked softly. Carys hesitated, then nodded. Julian reached out, his warm fingers brushing against hers as he took the barrel of the pen. A jolt of electricity shot up Carys’s arm, and she swallowed hard, suddenly hyper-aware of how close he was, the heat radiating from him in the damp chill of the shop. Julian’s demeanor had entirely shifted. The arrogant rogue was gone, replaced by a quiet, meticulous focus. He gently manipulated the internal spiral, his touch impossibly delicate. "You have to twist counter-clockwise as you push," he murmured, his voice dropping an octave. "It’s counterintuitive. Just like... well. A lot of things." He looked up through his wet eyelashes, meeting her gaze. The air in the dusty bookshop suddenly felt very thick. Carys couldn't look away. The hazel of her eyes locked onto the striking blue of his, the unspoken chemistry roaring into the silence between them. "There," Julian whispered, a soft click echoing in the quiet room. He didn't hand the pen back immediately. He held it, his eyes still fixed on hers. "Fixed." "Thank you," Carys managed to say, her usually sharp tongue completely failing her. Outside, the sound of shouting voices and splashing footsteps hurried past the frosted glass, fading down the cobblestone street. The paparazzi had moved on. Julian slowly set the pen down on the felt mat. He looked toward the door, then back at Carys, a slow, entirely genuine smile spreading across his face. It wasn't a movie-star smile. It was the smile of a man who had just found something incredibly rare. "They're gone," Carys said quietly.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates a meet-cute scenario in a Welsh bookstore with well-defined character archetypes - Julian as the charming but needy actor and Carys as the seemingly immune bookstore owner. The Welsh setting is established through specific details like the shop name "Llyfrau Arian," the "Ar Gau" sign, and references to Dylan Thomas and Welsh culture. The dialogue effectively carries the narrative as requested, with distinct voices for each character. Julian's speech patterns reflect his actor persona - initially performative and charm-laden, then shifting to genuine vulnerability and expertise. Carys maintains a cool, intellectual tone that masks her inner turmoil. The fountain pen subplot serves as an effective catalyst for genuine connection, moving beyond surface attraction to shared passion. Character development shows nuance, particularly in Julian's layers - the public persona, the fragile ego, and the hidden depth revealed through his fountain pen knowledge. Carys's internal conflict between attraction and resistance creates believable tension. The chemistry builds organically through their interaction rather than being forced. The prose quality is generally strong with evocative imagery like "rhythmic popping of camera flashes" and "treacherous flutter in her stomach." The pacing moves well from frantic opening to intimate conclusion. However, some descriptions veer toward purple prose territory with phrases like "entirely un-Welsh rhythm" and overly elaborate metaphors. The transformation from antagonistic strangers to mutual attraction feels earned through the fountain pen revelation, which provides genuine common ground. The ending maintains appropriate restraint - suggesting connection without rushing to romance. The piece successfully balances the requested character dynamics while maintaining emotional authenticity. Minor weaknesses include occasional overwrought descriptions and some predictable romantic beats, but the execution elevates familiar tropes through strong character work and dialogue. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 81.2
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Aya’s gloved hand closed around the cold brass wheel of the underhold door. It yielded with a groan, a deep, cetacean sound that reverberated through the damp stone of the Morska depths. She stepped over the threshold, Katra and Simeon close at her heels, their boots soft against the black-veined marble. The air inside tasted of ancient salt, refined spermaceti oil, and copper. The sputtering amber glow of the whale-oil lamps cast long, grasping shadows across the chamber, but the desperate chaos of the upper levels—the shouting, the shattering glass, the supposed raid—was entirely absent here. It was a suffocating, unnatural quiet. In that heavy silence, the frantic mechanism of Simeon’s pocket watch echoed like a hammer against an anvil from within his waistcoat. *Tick. Tick. Tick. Tick.* It marked each second with a mechanical indifference that set Aya’s teeth on edge. "Punctual. I’ve always admired that in an upstart," a voice rasped from the gloom. Aya froze. At the center of the chamber, resting upon a pedestal of wrought iron, sat the Scythan artifact, its crystalline surface inert. But seated directly in front of it, occupying a high-backed chair carved from the vertebrae of a deep-trench leviathan, was Madam Rusk. The Guild Leader was decidedly un-incarcerated. She wore a heavy coat of kraken-leather, a lit cigarillo clamped between her teeth, the cherry glowing like a demon’s eye in the smog. Flanking her were four enforcers, their faces obscured by soot-stained breathing masks. They did not wield the crude cudgels of street thugs; they carried heavy rotary-carbines, the polished brass and oiled steel gleaming in the dim light. *Tick. Tick. Tick. Tick.* "The raid," Aya breathed behind her velvet mask, her voice tight. "It was a ruse." "A necessary purge," Rusk corrected, exhaling a plume of noxious, sweet-smelling blubber-smoke. "To see which of my little rats would scurry for the cheese when they thought the cat was in a cage. Drop the iron, children. Slowly." Katra went rigid, her hands hovering instinctively over the twin trench-knives at her hips. Aya felt the sudden, violent churn of the *immateria* in her blood—the dark, formless void clawing at the edges of her consciousness, begging to be let out. She could feel the entities lurking in the peripheral shadows, whispering promises of blood and shadow. But this was a world of men who worshipped the absolute, brutal certainty of brass and lead. Magicka was a feared, unnatural heresy. To summon the void now would take three seconds; the rotary-carbines would tear her to red ribbons in one. "Do as she says," Aya commanded, her voice trembling only slightly. "No sudden movements." Simeon swallowed hard, carefully drawing a heavy revolver from his coat and letting it clatter to the stone. Katra followed suit, her knives ringing sharply against the marble. "Smart girl," Rusk murmured. She gestured with her cigarillo. "Gag the brute and the quiet one. Bring the shadow-weaver to me. Let’s see whose face is hiding behind my guild's recent string of successes." Two enforcers lunged forward, seizing Simeon and Katra. They offered no resistance as heavy iron cuffs were clamped around their wrists. A third enforcer grabbed Aya by the collar of her silken rogue’s coat, kicking the back of her knees. She hit the stone floor hard, a sharp gasp escaping her lips. Rough, calloused fingers dug into her hair, tearing the velvet mask from her face. The chill of the underhold hit Aya’s bare skin. Rusk leaned forward, the leviathan bone chair creaking. Her scarred, weather-beaten face shifted from cruel amusement to genuine shock. "Well, I'll be damned to the briny deep," the older woman breathed. "Lady Aya of House Valerius. A silk-sheet aristocrat playing in the muck." Panic, cold and absolute, seized Aya’s heart. *Ruin.* The word echoed in her mind, a death knell louder than Simeon's ceaseless watch. If her identity leaked to the surface, it would mean more than just the destruction of her family’s political standing. The Watch would search her manor. They would find her grimoires. They would find the arcane chalk, the vials of blackened blood, the forbidden texts of the *immateria*. She felt the phantom roughness of hemp rope wrapping around her throat. She saw her mother’s feet kicking at empty air, the snap of her neck echoing over the roar of the jeering Victorian crowds. That was the fate reserved for arcanists. That was the fate of a witch in a city of steel. *Tick. Tick. Tick. Tick.* "You look pale, Lady Valerius," Rusk mocked, standing up and walking slowly toward her, the heavy heels of her boots clicking against the stone. "Did you think this was a game? A bit of midnight theater to cure your aristocratic boredom?" "I think," Aya said, forcing her chin up, forcing the cultured, imperious cadence of her birthright past the terror closing her throat, "that you are a businesswoman, Madam Rusk. And as a businesswoman, you must recognize a sudden, massive shift in your assets." Rusk paused, a brow arching. "Is that so?" "If you kill me here, you get nothing but a corpse," Aya said, her mind racing, compartmentalizing the dread, locking the writhing *immateria* away in the darkest corner of her soul. "A corpse that will bring the entire City Watch, backed by my father’s private dragoons, down upon this underhold. They will tear the Morska guild apart brick by brick to find my murderers." "They wouldn't find you," one of the enforcers grunted, pressing the cold barrel of his carbine against her temple. "We'd feed you to the harbor threshers." "But if you keep me alive," Aya continued, ignoring the gun, locking her wide, dark eyes onto Rusk's. "If you hold me... I am the sole heir to the Valerius fortune. My ransom alone could fund this guild for a decade. Whale-oil rights, shipping lanes, unrestricted access to the upper-ward armories. I can give you all of it." Simeon shifted uncomfortably against his captor's grip. *Tick. Tick. Tick. Tick.* "You're bargaining with your own kidnapping," Rusk mused, a slow, predatory smile spreading across her face. "You're offering to bankrupt your own father to save your skin." "I am offering you a transaction," Aya said, her voice dropping to a desperate, hardened whisper. "You deal in coin and leverage. I am the greatest piece of leverage you will ever hold. But only if my identity remains a secret. Only if I breathe." Rusk stared down at her for a long, agonizing moment. The smell of gun-oil and unwashed bodies pressed in on Aya. The shadows in the corners of the room seemed to stretch and curl, the immateria feeding on her raw, suppressed terror. Finally, Rusk chuckled, a wet, rattling sound. She reached down, tapping the hot cherry of her cigarillo against Aya’s cheek, just hard enough to burn. Aya flinched, but held her ground. "Lock them in the deep-water cells," Rusk commanded, turning her back to the trio. "And take the watch off the boy. The ticking is giving me a migraine." As the enforcers hauled her roughly to her feet, Aya’s gaze lingered on the Scythan artifact, and then drifted to the impenetrable shadows of the ceiling. She had bought herself time, but the cost of her double life had finally come due. She was a prisoner of the underworld, a hostage to her own secrets, and the gallows loomed ever closer in the dark.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and atmospheric worldbuilding within the "whalepunk" fantasy setting. The author successfully creates a vivid Victorian-gothic atmosphere through detailed descriptions of whale-oil lamps, kraken-leather coats, and leviathan bone furniture. The worldbuilding feels cohesive and immersive, with consistent internal logic about the tension between industrial technology and feared magic. The adherence to instructions is excellent - all story beats are hit precisely: the trio enters the underhold, finds the un-incarcerated guild leader, Aya is captured and unmasked, and she attempts to negotiate using her value as a hostage. The dialogue maintains appropriate period voice and the third-person perspective with internal monologue is well-executed. Character actions feel believable throughout. Aya's quick thinking in offering herself as a valuable hostage rather than a mere victim demonstrates intelligence and desperation in equal measure. Her internal struggle between her aristocratic identity and criminal activities creates compelling tension. Rusk emerges as a credible antagonist - cunning, ruthless, and appropriately theatrical. The prose quality is generally strong, with evocative imagery like "the frantic mechanism of Simeon's pocket watch echoed like a hammer against an anvil" and "the cherry glowing like a demon's eye in the smog." However, there are moments where the descriptive language becomes slightly overwrought, particularly in the opening paragraphs with phrases like "deep, cetacean sound" and "black-veined marble." The dialogue feels authentic to the setting and characters. Rusk's voice is particularly well-crafted with lines like "A necessary purge... to see which of my little rats would scurry for the cheese when they thought the cat was in a cage." Aya's shift between desperate fear and aristocratic negotiation feels natural. The emotional complexity is well-handled, particularly Aya's terror at exposure and the phantom sensation of the noose around her neck, connecting to her mother's execution. This creates genuine stakes and emotional resonance. Some weaknesses include occasional purple prose tendencies and a few moments where description overwhelms action. The piece also relies heavily on atmospheric description at the expense of character development for Katra and Simeon, who remain somewhat underdeveloped. The ending, while appropriately dark, feels somewhat predictable in its trajectory. + Fanfic-crossover: Gunpowder Rendezvous — Score: 37.8
Prompt:
Gunpowder Rendezvous Spike Spiegel & Revy Write a story where Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop) and Revy (Black Lagoon) meet in a seedy bar after both being hired for the same bounty. Their mutual attraction is complicated by their trust issues and competitive instincts. Include drinking, gunplay, and unresolved sexual tension that culminates in a motel room standoff. **Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop)** Spike is a laid-back bounty hunter with a dark past as a syndicate enforcer. He practices Jeet Kune Do and is exceptionally skilled with firearms. He's chronically broke, constantly hungry, and addicted to cigarettes. Despite his carefree attitude, he's haunted by his past and the woman he left behind. He has a prosthetic eye from a past injury that affects his depth perception. He's fatalistic to the extreme, often saying he's already died once. He values freedom above all else and dislikes attachments, though he secretly cares deeply for his crew. Famous quote: "Whatever happens, happens." **Revy/Rebecca Lee (Black Lagoon)** Revy is a Chinese-American dual-wielding gunslinger with unparalleled shooting skills. She's foul-mouthed, chain-smoking, and has severe anger management issues stemming from childhood abuse. She's known as "Two Hands" for her ability to accurately fire two pistols simultaneously. She drinks heavily and solves most problems with violence. Despite her hardened exterior, she has moments of vulnerability, particularly around Rock. She has a dragon tattoo covering her right arm and typically wears extremely short shorts and a black crop top regardless of the situation. Famous quote: "I'm not a good person, not a bad person. I'm just here." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
A ring of rust on Spike’s lighter suggests it’s been dropped in water more than once. He flicks the wheel, coaxing a meager yellow flame to the tip of a crumpled cigarette. The smoke fills his lungs, a temporary substitute for the bell peppers and beef he absolutely cannot afford. His stomach gives a hollow rumble. He’s chronically broke, and the dregs of cheap whiskey in his glass aren't helping to take the edge off. The dive bar smells of stale beer, sweat, and impending violence. Spike leans against the sticky mahogany, his mismatched eyes scanning the room. His right eye—a prosthetic that occasionally throws his depth perception out of whack—catches a flash of ink through the dim, hazy lighting. She sits two stools down
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This response is severely incomplete, cutting off mid-sentence after only about 150 words when 1000 words were requested. The fragment that exists shows some promise in establishing atmosphere and character details, but fails to deliver on virtually all the prompt requirements. The opening demonstrates competent scene-setting with sensory details like the "ring of rust" on Spike's lighter and the bar's atmosphere of "stale beer, sweat, and impending violence." The writing incorporates character-specific details well, referencing Spike's chronic hunger, poverty, smoking habit, and prosthetic eye. The prose style is appropriately noir-ish and matches the tone expected for these characters. However, the response fails catastrophically in execution. It doesn't include the meeting between Spike and Revy, their conversation, the bounty conflict, drinking scenes, gunplay, sexual tension, or the motel standoff - essentially none of the core story elements requested. Revy is barely introduced before the text cuts off. There's no dialogue, no character interaction, and no plot development. The writing that exists is competent but unremarkable. The imagery is functional rather than striking, and while it avoids purple prose, it also lacks distinctive voice or memorable turns of phrase. The incomplete nature makes it impossible to assess most narrative elements like character development, emotional complexity, or story structure. + Folk horror: Útiseta — Score: 44.3
Prompt:
Folk Horror: Útiseta Write a chapter set in rural northern Sweden in the mid-1990s. Linnea, a teenage girl from Stockholm, has reluctantly moved to a small village with her parents after her father's job transfer. She's befriended Astrid, a local girl who claims her grandmother taught her ancient practices passed down through generations. Astrid has been introducing Linnea to "midnight games" that involve old rituals, convincing her they can use them to learn classmates' secrets or influence their behavior. Tonight, Astrid has convinced Linnea to participate in Útiseta—sitting out on an ancient burial mound at midnight to "receive knowledge from beyond." The chapter focuses on this single midnight excursion, the ritualistic preparations, Astrid's matter-of-fact instructions that mix teenage rebellion with eerily specific traditional elements, and the mounting tension as Linnea becomes increasingly uncertain whether they're simply scaring themselves or if something is actually responding to their presence. The narrative should maintain ambiguity about whether the strange occurrences are supernatural or psychological, while establishing the dangerous line they're crossing by treating ancient practices as entertainment. First person, past tense, told as Linnea's recollection of events in the present day (though this device should be largely transparent). 1000 words. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
Looking back, I can blame the isolation. When my father’s engineering firm relocated us from the neon-lit, perpetual hum of Stockholm to a nameless clutch of timber houses in northern Sweden, the sheer silence of Norrland felt like a physical weight pressing against my eardrums. It was late October of 1995, and the dark was already swallowing the afternoons by three o'clock. I was fourteen, angry at the world, and desperate for an anchor in this frozen expanse. Astrid became that anchor. She smelled consistently of damp wool, roll-on vanilla deodorant, and woodsmoke. She was a local girl whose grandmother had supposedly taught her the old ways—traditions passed down through generations of women who survived the brutal winters by knowing things they shouldn
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This response is severely truncated, cutting off mid-sentence after only about 150 words of what should be a 1000-word piece. The fragment that exists shows promise in several areas but cannot be properly evaluated as a complete work. The opening demonstrates strong atmospheric writing with evocative details like "neon-lit, perpetual hum of Stockholm" contrasted against the "frozen expanse" of northern Sweden. The narrator's voice feels authentic for a teenage girl reflecting on past events, and the specific sensory details about Astrid ("damp wool, roll-on vanilla deodorant, and woodsmoke") show skilled character development through concrete imagery. The setting establishment is effective, capturing both the temporal specificity (October 1995) and the psychological displacement of an urban teenager thrust into rural isolation. The phrase "the dark was already swallowing the afternoons by three o'clock" effectively conveys both literal and metaphorical darkness. However, the response fails catastrophically in meeting the basic requirement of length and completeness. None of the central elements requested—the midnight ritual, the burial mound, the Útiseta practice, the mounting tension, or the ambiguous supernatural elements—are present. The narrative hasn't even begun to approach the core dramatic situation described in the prompt. The prose style, while competent in the existing fragment, cannot be fully assessed for consistency, pacing, or development. The emotional complexity and folk horror elements that should define this piece are entirely absent due to the truncation. + Physical-spatial: Therapeutic Boundaries — Score: 81.8
Prompt:
**"Therapeutic Boundaries"** Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a former professional athlete undergoing mandatory physical therapy after a career-destroying injury, harboring resentment toward recovery itself. Their physiotherapist is not only unnervingly attractive but also a former training partner from their competitive days who has successfully transitioned to a new career while the protagonist's identity remains shattered. We'll lean into the sexual tension of the scene by having her be attracted to him, but both of them attempting to maintain mental distance while being in physical contact as he shows her stretches & movements & manipulates her body, navigating the thresholds of pain. This scene MUST maintain complete physical coherence—every movement, stretch, and manipulation must be anatomically precise and spatially consistent. Paint an exceptionally vivid sensory picture through detailed attention to pain thresholds, involuntary responses to touch. Capture the suppressed, flaring tension of physical proximity, touch, and the controlled infliction of uncomfortable movements. Physical clarity is essential — readers must understand exactly what is happening with the body during each therapeutic technique. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The sanitary paper beneath me crinkles like dry leaves, a harsh, brittle sound that echoes the state of my right knee. I stare at the clinic’s fluorescent ceiling panels, nursing a cold, leaden resentment toward the very concept of healing. Healing implies a return to form. But a completely ruptured patellar tendon and a shredded medial meniscus don’t just heal; they leave you a hollowed-out civilian. I don’t want to recover. Recovery is an admission that my career as an elite hurdler is dead, and the body I spent twenty years sharpening into a weapon is now just a broken liability. Then Julian walks in, and the resentment twists into something far more complicated. He looks unnervingly good. Too good for a man who used to vomit beside me into track-side trash cans after 400-meter repeats. While my identity shattered on the tartan track in Eugene, Julian gracefully pivoted. Now, he is Dr. Julian Hayes, a highly sought-after physiotherapist. Beneath his clinical navy polo, he wears the faded, geometric crimson-and-charcoal compression sleeves we bought in bulk before the 2019 Olympic Trials. Seeing them feels like a physical blow. They are a ghost of our shared past, wrapping his forearms in a life I have been violently exiled from. "Ready?" he asks, his voice a smooth, professional baritone that completely ignores the heavy, suffocating history between us. "Just get it over with," I mutter. I am lying supine. Julian steps to the foot of the table. His large, warm hands close over my right leg—his left palm cupping my heel, his right hand sliding beneath the crook of my knee. Even through the chill of the air-conditioned room, the heat of his skin sears through my thin athletic shorts. My pulse immediately jumps, an involuntary flutter in my throat that I furiously try to suppress. He begins with passive hip flexion, slowly elevating my straightened leg toward the ceiling to lengthen the atrophied hamstring. The movement is flawlessly smooth, mechanically precise. As the angle of my leg passes forty-five degrees, the rusted cables of my biceps femoris pull taut. "Knee straight. Breathe," he commands softly. He steps closer to the side of the table, closing the physical gap between us. To gain leverage, he lifts my leg higher, guiding my heel to rest directly against his chest, right over his pectoral muscle. The intimacy of the position is jarring. I am forced to feel the steady, rhythmic rise and fall of his breathing against my ankle. I stare resolutely at his shoulder, refusing to meet his eyes, desperately trying to maintain a mental wall of clinical detachment. "We’re doing PNF today," Julian says, his thumb pressing firmly into the medial aspect of my knee to keep the joint locked. "When I say go, I want you to contract the hamstring. Drive your heel into my chest with about twenty percent of your max effort. Hold for five seconds." "I know how Proprioceptive Neuromuscular Facilitation works, Julian," I snap, the bitterness leaking through. "Then go," he replies, unbothered. I engage the muscle. I press my heel into the solid, unyielding wall of his chest. The proximity is intoxicating. I can smell the sharp, clinical alcohol of the clinic blended with the deep, familiar scent of cedarwood and his faint, natural sweat. As I push, the muscle fibers in my thigh tremble violently. My body, starved for the adrenaline of competition and the dopamine of human touch, betrays me with a sudden, involuntary shiver that ripples up my spine. "Relax," he murmurs. The moment my muscle yields, he leans his body weight forward, driving his chest against my foot to force my leg into a drastically deeper stretch. A white-hot bloom of pain explodes at the ischial tuberosity, right where the hamstring anchors to my pelvis. My breath hitches sharply, a ragged gasp tearing from my throat. My hands instantly fist into the crinkling paper. Julian freezes. His eyes dart down to mine. For a fraction of a second, the professional mask slips. His pupils are dilated, dark and entirely too focused on my flushed face. The air in the room suddenly feels thick, charged with a flaring, suppressed tension. We are inches apart, connected by a rigid line of agonizing tension and undeniable heat. He is hurting me, precisely and deliberately, and heaven help me, my body is flushing with a heavy, pooling warmth that has absolutely nothing to do with inflammation. "Too much?" he asks, his voice dropping an octave, losing its clinical polish. "No," I whisper, my voice trembling. "Hold it." He holds the stretch for thirty agonizing, electric seconds before slowly lowering my leg back to the table. The rush of blood back into the joint leaves my skin tingling. "Roll onto your left side," he instructs, clearing his throat. "We need to release the IT band." I turn, bending my right knee and letting it rest on a foam bolster. The iliotibial tract running down the outside of my thigh is a dense, inflamed sheet of scar tissue. Julian steps up behind me. I can feel the radiating heat of his torso hovering just inches from my back. He doesn't use his hands this time. He lowers his forearm, the crimson-and-charcoal compression sleeve brushing against the bare skin of my lower thigh. The friction of the synthetic fabric sends a cascade of goosebumps across my flesh. He sinks the point of his elbow directly into the tensor fasciae latae, the muscular anchor at the top of my hip, and begins a slow, excruciatingly deep downward glide toward my knee. The pain is blinding—a sharp, fibrous tearing sensation that threatens to crack my composure in half. I bite down hard on my lower lip, tasting copper. My hips involuntarily buck backward, an instinctual retreat from the pain, pushing my glutes flush against the front of his thighs. Julian’s breath catches audibly. He stops the downward glide of his elbow but doesn't pull away. We are frozen in a brutally intimate alignment, my back pressed to his front, his elbow buried deep in the muscle of my hip. I can feel the rigid tension in his legs, the sudden, rigid stillness of his body. He is fighting the exact same battle I am—trying to separate the athlete from the woman, the therapist from the man, the pain from the desire. Slowly, deliberately, he eases the pressure of his elbow, sliding his arm off my leg. He takes a distinct step backward, putting sterile, clinical space between us. "We'll stop there for today," Julian says, his voice tight, carefully controlled. I roll onto my back, chest heaving, my skin still burning where he touched me. I look up at him, entirely ruined, while he stands there, perfectly whole, adjusting the sleeves of a past I can never return to.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution in several key areas while maintaining the delicate balance required by the prompt's complex demands. The narrative successfully establishes the protagonist's psychological state - a former elite hurdler whose identity has been shattered by career-ending injury. The resentment toward recovery is palpable and well-motivated, creating authentic internal conflict. The physical therapy sequences are anatomically precise and spatially coherent, fulfilling one of the prompt's most demanding requirements. The descriptions of passive hip flexion, PNF stretching, and IT band release are technically accurate and clearly visualized. The writer demonstrates genuine understanding of physiotherapy techniques while maintaining narrative flow. The sexual tension is handled with sophistication, building through physical proximity and shared history rather than explicit content. The compression sleeves serve as an effective symbolic bridge between their past and present, adding layers of meaning to their interaction. The power dynamics are complex - he holds professional authority while she experiences physical vulnerability, yet both are fighting similar internal battles. The sensory writing is particularly strong, with precise attention to pain thresholds, involuntary physical responses, and the interplay between discomfort and desire. The protagonist's body betraying her emotional defenses creates authentic conflict. The clinical setting provides effective contrast to the underlying tension. Character voice remains consistent throughout - bitter, resistant, yet vulnerable. The protagonist's expertise (knowing PNF techniques) maintains believability while her emotional responses feel genuine. Julian's professional composure gradually cracking adds realistic dimension. The prose occasionally ventures toward purple territory with phrases like "white-hot bloom of pain" and "brutally intimate alignment," but generally maintains elegance. The ending avoids false resolution while providing emotional payoff - both characters remain in their respective states of wholeness and brokenness, which feels earned given the setup. Minor weaknesses include some overwrought moments and a few instances where the technical precision slightly interrupts narrative flow. The piece could have explored the protagonist's athletic identity loss more deeply, though space constraints may have limited this development. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 7 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Historical fiction: High Seas and Low Vices — Score: 82.2
Prompt:
High Seas and Low Vices: British Naval Officer in the Opium Trade Character: Lieutenant James Merrick, a high-ranking officer in the British Royal Navy stationed in Canton, China in the 1830s. Once promising and decorated, now struggling with a severe opium addiction he hides from his superiors. Synopsis: Write a chapter following Lieutenant Merrick during a single day as he shirks his official naval duties in what has become a daily need to satiate the dragon. Told primarily in dialogue with the characters he interacts with. Focus on brief slices of his journey through the shadowy underworld of Canton: interactions with acquaintances, negotiations with local dealers, schemes and unsavoury jobs taken to fund his habit. Writing Task: Use rich sensory details to describe a day in the life of James Merrick. Write for a real sense of time & place while avoiding cliche. First person, past tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The spasm hit me just as my boots hit the quarterdeck of HMS *Tamar*. I doubled over the polished oak rail, a violent racking that rattled my teeth. When I pulled my linen handkerchief away from my mouth, it was dotted with dark, wet crimson. The back of my throat tasted sharply of old copper pennies. "Lieutenant Merrick? Are you quite well, sir?" I hastily crumpled the linen into my uniform pocket and turned to face Midshipman Hayes. The boy’s face was a picture of naval innocence, framed by the oppressive, glaring humidity of the morning sun over the Pearl River. "Perfectly well, Mr. Hayes," I rasped, forcing my spine straight. I swallowed hard against the metallic tang. "It is merely the sea air. This infernal dampness settles in the lungs." "Shall I fetch the surgeon, sir? Captain Elliot wishes to review the blockade logs at four bells." "You will do no such thing," I snapped, harsher than intended. "I have urgent business ashore. The hong merchants are disputing our anchorage fees again. Tell the Captain I am handling it." "But sir, the logs—" "I am handling it, Hayes. See to your duties." I did not wait for his salute. I practically threw myself down the gangway into the waiting gig, desperate to escape the pristine order of the ship. By the time I reached the muddy docks of the Thirteen Factories, the Canton heat was a physical weight. The air was a suffocating soup of rotting fish, spilled jasmine tea, and the raw sewage that bobbed in the shallows. I pushed through a throng of shouting coolies and hawkers in Hog Lane, the coppery taste in my mouth growing thicker with every step. I found Silas Vance in the shadowed corner of a dilapidated tea house, nursing a cup of cheap, fermented samshu. Vance was a privateer in name and a parasite in practice. "You look ghastly, James," Vance said, not bothering to rise as I slid onto the wooden bench opposite him. "Like a corpse left out to dry." "Spare me the pleasantries, Silas. Do you have the silver?" "That depends entirely upon whether you have the charts." Vance smiled, revealing teeth stained brown by tobacco and betel nut. I glanced around the dim, chattering room. Satisfied no one from the squadron was present, I reached into my coat and slid a folded piece of parchment across the sticky table. "The patrol routes for the next fortnight," I murmured, my voice trembling slightly. "The frigate *Volage* is taking the eastern channel. The western delta will be completely unmonitored by the Crown until the fourteenth." Vance unfolded the paper, his eyes darting over my meticulous, traitorous ink strokes. "And the revenue cutters?" "Anchored for repairs at Macao. You have a clear run for your clippers." Another cough bubbled up in my chest. I stifled it into my fist, trying to swallow the blood. "You're coughing again, Lieutenant," Vance noted, his eyes narrowing with a predator's amusement. "It is the sea air," I said, my voice tight. "Nothing more. The money, Silas." He chuckled, tossing a heavy leather purse onto the table. It clinked with the beautiful, heavy sound of Spanish silver dollars. "Two hundred. As agreed. Try not to spend it all in one alleyway, James. The Crown might notice her decorated golden boy has gone to rust." I grabbed the purse and left without another word. The silver burned a hole in my pocket as I navigated the labyrinthine alleys behind the foreign quarter. The sun was dipping lower now, casting long, bruised shadows over the brickwork. I reached the water's edge, where a cluster of floating slums—tethered sampans—bobbed on the murky tide. I stepped onto the deck of a weathered junk, the wood groaning beneath my boots. "Master Chen," I called out into the darkened cabin. The smell of the river mud was entirely overpowered here by a sweet, cloying aroma. It smelled like burnt molasses and dried flowers. It smelled like salvation. A skeletal Chinese man emerged from the gloom, his eyes milky in the dim light. "Lieutenant," Chen hissed softly. "You are back soon. You smoke too much, I think." "I am not paying for your medical opinion," I said, tossing Vance’s purse onto a bamboo mat. "I need Patna. Three balls. And I need a pipe prepared immediately." Chen weighed the purse in his long, spindly hands. "Silver is good. But the Mandarins, they search the riverboats today. Very dangerous. The price of Patna is higher." "Do not play games with me, Chen. Take the silver and prepare the pipe, or I will have a squadron of marines burn this floating hovel to the waterline." Chen’s smile was a thin, humorless line. "No marines come here, Lieutenant. You bring them, they see you. They see the great British officer trembling like a starved dog." My hand flew to the hilt of my naval dirk, but another cough seized me. It tore through my chest, violent and wet. I spat onto the deck; the globule was bright red in the fading light. The coppery taste was overwhelming, making me gag. "The sea air?" Chen mocked softly, gesturing toward the back of the cabin. "Go. Lie down. I bring the pipe." I stumbled into the cramped, windowless hold. The heat was stifling, the air thick with the ghosts of a thousand exhales. I collapsed onto a stained silk pillow, my uniform jacket—the dark blue wool and brass buttons of His Majesty’s Navy—bunching uncomfortably around my shoulders. Moments later, Chen knelt beside me. He held a long bamboo pipe and a small oil lamp. With a long needle, he roasted a dark, sticky pellet of opium over the flame. It bubbled and hissed, expanding into a golden-brown blister. "Breathe deep, Lieutenant," Chen whispered, pressing the mouthpiece to my lips. I closed my eyes and inhaled. The thick, sweet smoke flooded my lungs. Almost instantly, the violent trembling in my hands ceased. The heavy, suffocating heat of Canton melted away, replaced by a cool, rushing euphoria that untethered me from the wooden planks, from the ship, from the treason I had committed. "More," I breathed out, my voice finally steady. Chen smiled in the dark, already rolling the next pellet over the flame. "Of course, Lieutenant." I lay back in the shadows, waiting for the dragon to pull me under. For a little while, at least, I could no longer taste the copper.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and atmospheric writing within the historical fiction genre. The author successfully creates an immersive 1830s Canton setting through carefully chosen sensory details - the "suffocating soup of rotting fish, spilled jasmine tea, and raw sewage," the "oppressive, glaring humidity," and the tactile descriptions of the floating slums. The historical context feels authentic without being heavy-handed. The characterization of Lieutenant Merrick is particularly well-handled. His addiction is portrayed with unflinching realism - the physical symptoms (coughing blood, trembling hands), the psychological desperation, and the moral degradation (selling naval intelligence) all feel authentic. The progression from his attempt to maintain naval propriety with Midshipman Hayes to his complete surrender in Chen's opium den creates a compelling character arc within the single day structure. The dialogue serves multiple functions effectively. Vance's predatory amusement ("Like a corpse left out to dry") and Chen's calculated manipulation show how Merrick's addiction has made him vulnerable to exploitation. The conversations reveal character relationships and advance the plot while maintaining period-appropriate speech patterns. The prose strikes a good balance between literary quality and accessibility. Phrases like "the dragon to pull me under" and "decorated golden boy has gone to rust" are evocative without being overwrought. The sensory writing is particularly strong - the metallic taste of blood, the sweet cloying aroma of opium, the physical weight of Canton's heat. However, some elements feel slightly predictable. The trajectory from naval duty to betrayal to opium den follows an expected path, though it's executed well. The ending, while thematically appropriate, doesn't offer much surprise beyond the competent execution of the premise. The piece maintains consistent pacing and voice throughout, with Merrick's desperation driving the narrative forward naturally. The author avoids common pitfalls like excessive exposition or melodrama, instead letting the character's actions and physical deterioration speak for themselves. + Humour: Sisyphus Gives Advice — Score: 84.5
Prompt:
Sisyphus Gives Advice Write a comedic scene in the style of Terry Pratchett featuring Sisyphus, who has started an advice column while continuing his eternal boulder-pushing. Rather than being bitter about his punishment, Sisyphus has developed a comfortably zen emotional intelligence and wisdom from his centuries of repetitive labor. Not in the cliche ways you might expect -- he's actually very grounded, warm & connected with the everyday human plight. He dictates responses to "Crow," his feathered familiar who serves as both scribe and mail carrier. The piece should include Sisyphus thoughtfully answering several advice-seekers' letters about everyday problems (relationship troubles, work-life balance, dealing with difficult people, life purpose doubts) while periodically returning to his boulder-pushing duties. His advice should be surprisingly insightful and practical. The humor should emerge naturally from the messages he receives, from mundane to risque. Sisyphus should genuinely enjoy helping others, finding meaning in this new pursuit while maintaining his boulder routine. The humour should be *understated and not forced*. Contrary to expectation, almost none of his advice involves boulder rolling metaphors (though he does sneak *one* subtle in there), nor a treatise on stoicism. We are also going against the obvious fish-out-of-water trope; in fact, he's surprisingly a natural at this. In all of this , it's important to *show, don't tell*. The format should be largely epistolary, with the occasional interjection for scene setting. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Tartarus was not, traditionally, known for its robust postal service.[1] It was mostly known for the sulfur, the groaning, and the kind of existential dread that really ruins a good weekend. But Sisyphus had found that eternity, much like a stubborn jar of olives, simply required the right angle of approach. He leaned his shoulder into the three-ton sphere of granite, his calves flexing with the practiced, casual grace of a man who had been doing exactly this since before the invention of the weekend. He breathed in, exhaled, and pushed. A few yards to his left, keeping pace by hopping from crag to crag, was Crow. Crow wore a tiny leather satchel and had a piece of chalk firmly clamped in his beak. He pecked rhythmically at a slate tablet, taking dictation. "Read the next one, please," Sisyphus said, his voice entirely devoid of strain. Crow dropped the chalk, pulled a crumpled piece of parchment from his satchel, and cleared his throat with a sound like a rusty hinge. *"Dear Sisyphus,"* Crow croaked. *"My husband of five years has recently discovered ‘mindfulness’ and now insists on chewing every bite of his dinner exactly forty-two times. Meals take hours. I love him, but I am contemplating light poisoning. Sincerely, Starving in Sparta."* Sisyphus paused to wipe a bead of sweat from his brow, wedging his knee under the boulder to hold it in place. He considered the slate. "Dear Starving," Sisyphus dictated, his tone warm and conversational. "Poisoning, while traditional, leaves a terrible mess, and the legal fees are astronomical. The issue here isn't the chewing; it's the captive audience. Eat your own dinner at a normal pace, then use his mastication time to take up a hobby. I recommend watercolor painting, or perhaps learning the lute. When he realizes he is chewing to an empty room, the novelty will likely wear off. If it doesn't, at least you'll know how to play the lute." Crow nodded approvingly, the chalk scratching furiously against the slate. Sisyphus shifted his weight and resumed his upward trudge. The stone rolled over a patch of loose shale. Sisyphus didn't curse; he simply adjusted his footing, anticipating the slip before it happened. Centuries of repetitive labor hadn't broken him; they had simply stripped away all the unnecessary emotional baggage, leaving behind a man who was extremely good at walking uphill and highly attuned to the mechanics of getting stuck. *"Next letter,"* Crow announced, pulling out a slightly damp scroll. *"Dear Sisyphus. I am an apprentice sandal-maker. My master expects me to stay late to sweep the workshop, polish the lasts, and feed his awful cat, all unpaid. He says it's ‘character building.’ I am exhausted. Tired in Troy."* Before Sisyphus could answer, a cloud of cheap incense materialized on the path ahead. Out stepped Narthex, the minor deity of Middle Management and Unsolicited Feedback. He wore a pristine toga and held a clipboard. "You're losing your footing on the left side," Narthex pointed out, tapping the clipboard with a stylus. "And your engagement metrics are down. You should tell the sandal-maker to endure. Suffering builds the soul. Look at you! A shining example of corporate synergy." Sisyphus smiled, a genuine, crinkly-eyed expression. "Hello, Narthex. Mind your toes." Narthex leaped back as the boulder rumbled past his sandals. "I'm just saying, Sisyphus, this advice column is a distraction from your Core Deliverables." "Thank you for your perspective, Narthex," Sisyphus said pleasantly, not breaking his stride. "Have a wonderful afternoon." Narthex huffed, muttered something about 'insubordination,' and vanished in a puff of bureaucracy. "Where were we?" Sisyphus asked. "Ah, yes. Dear Tired. 'Character building' is merely management-speak for 'free labor.' Your master's cat is not your responsibility. Set a firm boundary. Tomorrow, say: 'I am available for feline maintenance at my standard overtime rate.' People treat you the way you train them to treat you. If he fires you, good. Sandal-making is a booming industry; take your awl
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive style, successfully capturing his blend of whimsy, philosophical depth, and understated humor. The author effectively establishes Sisyphus as a surprisingly grounded advice columnist who has found genuine purpose in helping others while maintaining his eternal punishment. The characterization of Sisyphus is particularly well-executed. Rather than the expected bitter or overly philosophical figure, he emerges as warmly practical and emotionally intelligent. His interactions feel natural and his advice is genuinely insightful without being preachy. The relationship with Crow as scribe works well, providing both practical narrative function and gentle humor. The epistolary format is handled skillfully, with the advice letters feeling authentic and varied. The problems presented (mindful chewing husband, exploitative master) are relatable and allow Sisyphus to demonstrate his wisdom in concrete, practical ways. His responses show genuine care and understanding of human nature. The prose captures Pratchett's voice admirably, particularly in observations like "eternity, much like a stubborn jar of olives, simply required the right angle of approach" and the description of Tartarus's postal service. The humor emerges naturally from character interactions and situations rather than forced jokes. The introduction of Narthex as a middle management deity is inspired and very Pratchett-esque, providing both comic relief and demonstrating Sisyphus's evolved emotional maturity in how he handles the interruption with grace. However, the piece feels incomplete, cutting off mid-sentence during the second advice response. This prevents full assessment of the work's arc and resolution. The writing occasionally borders on being slightly too conscious of its Pratchett imitation, though it generally succeeds in feeling authentic rather than pastiche. The author successfully avoids the obvious pitfalls mentioned in the prompt - there are no heavy-handed boulder metaphors, no fish-out-of-water humor, and Sisyphus genuinely excels at his new role. The "show don't tell" instruction is largely followed, with character traits emerging through actions and dialogue rather than exposition. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 17 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 16 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 2 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Coming of age: Lost & Found in Osaka — Score: 44.8
Prompt:
Lost & Found in Osaka Slice of Life / Coming-of-Age: First Connection. Write one scene within a larger story set in Osaka, Japan. A shy, introverted teenager & synthesizer nerd, is browsing the electronics district of Den Den Town. She is drawn to the sound of an electric guitar. A girl, slightly older and radiating confidence, is busking near the entrance to a crowded arcade, playing a blistering rendition of a classic anime theme. The scene takes us through their initial awkward interaction, transitioning into a surprisingly easy conversation. The busker, intrigued by the teenager's quiet intensity and knowledge of music, suggests they grab some sushi at a nearby kaitenzushi (conveyor belt sushi) restaurant. Write primarily in dialogue, capturing the distinct voices of the two characters: the teenager's hesitant and -- initially -- awkward speech, and the busker's more outgoing and playful tone. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The neon glow of Den Den Town fought a losing battle against the fading evening light, but the noise was the real spectacle. The electronic pulse of pachinko parlors, the synthesized cheer of crane games, and the thumping bass from rhythm arcade cabinets usually bled together into a chaotic, comforting static for Rei. Today, however, a sharp, biting melody cut straight through the din. Rei stopped, her hand instinctively tightening around the strap of her backpack—heavy with newly purchased patch cables and a vintage Roland drum machine manual. She turned toward the entrance of Club SEGA. A girl, maybe nineteen or twenty, was planted on the pavement, wrestling a blistering solo out of a battered Fiesta Red Stratocaster. She leaned back, eyes closed, her fingers flying across the
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This response is severely incomplete, cutting off mid-sentence after only about 150 words when 1000 words were requested. The piece establishes a promising opening with vivid sensory details of Den Den Town's electronic atmosphere and introduces the protagonist Rei effectively. The writing demonstrates competent prose with good imagery - the "neon glow fighting a losing battle against fading evening light" and the layered soundscape create an immersive setting. However, the response fails catastrophically in meeting the core requirements. The prompt explicitly calls for a scene "written primarily in dialogue" capturing the "distinct voices of the two characters" through their interaction, conversation, and eventual trip to a sushi restaurant. None of this occurs. There is no dialogue whatsoever, no interaction between characters, and no progression beyond the initial setup. The characterization of Rei shows promise - her physical reaction of tightening her backpack strap suggests nervousness, and the detail about her carrying music equipment establishes her as the synthesizer enthusiast described in the prompt. The busker is barely introduced before the text cuts off. The prose quality is solid but not exceptional. Phrases like "chaotic, comforting static" work well, though "battered Fiesta Red Stratocaster" veers slightly toward unnecessary brand specificity. The writing flows naturally and avoids purple prose or overwrought language. The fundamental issue is incompleteness - this reads like the opening paragraphs of what could have been a successful response to the prompt, but it delivers perhaps 15% of what was requested.
